patient_information.name: Michael Aaron Thompson
patient_information.allergies: Allergic to penicillin (develops generalized pruritic rash and hives, no anaphylaxis). Seasonal allergic rhinitis triggered by grass and ragweed pollens causing nasal congestion, sneezing, and itchy eyes each spring and fall. Reports mild sensitivity to cat dander with wheezing and chest tightness after prolonged exposure; avoids cats when possible.

physician_information.address_physician: 1120 S Utica Ave, Suite 450, Tulsa, OK 74104

nucala_order.nucala_order: Nucala (mepolizumab) subcutaneous injections to be administered in infusion center per dosing selected below.

required_diagnosis.stat_reason: Patient with biopsy and laboratory-confirmed eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) characterized by adult-onset asthma, chronic sinusitis with nasal polyps, peripheral eosinophilia (>1500 cells/µL on multiple occasions), and biopsy-proven eosinophilic vasculitis involving skin and peripheral nerves. Over the past 12 months he has required three hospitalizations for severe asthma exacerbations and vasculitic flares, including one ICU admission in August 2025 for status asthmaticus and hypoxic respiratory failure. Despite maximized inhaled therapy (high-dose ICS/LABA, LAMA, leukotriene receptor antagonist) and chronic low-dose oral prednisone (currently 10 mg daily with frequent bursts up to 40 mg), he continues to experience frequent wheezing, nocturnal awakenings, and activity limitation, with an ACT score consistently <15. He has developed steroid-related side effects including weight gain, hypertension, and impaired fasting glucose. Nucala is being initiated to achieve better control of eosinophilic inflammation, reduce exacerbation frequency, permit tapering of systemic corticosteroids, and prevent further end-organ damage from vasculitis. While this is not marked as a STAT request, timely initiation within the next 2–4 weeks is medically important to minimize risk of additional exacerbations and further cumulative steroid toxicity.

standing_lab_orders.lab_frequency: Obtain CBC with differential and CMP at baseline (prior to or at first Nucala administration), then every 6 months while on therapy, and additionally as clinically indicated if there is concern for drug-related adverse effects, worsening eosinophilia, or new systemic symptoms suggestive of vasculitic flare. Fax results to ordering physician within 48 hours of availability.

notes__additional_comments.notes_additional_comments_line_1: Patient is a 44-year-old non-smoker with a 7-year history of adult-onset asthma that progressively evolved into eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis. He works as an IT project manager and has two school-aged children at home. Asthma symptoms are worse during spring and fall but he now experiences year-round cough, chest tightness, and exertional dyspnea, particularly when climbing stairs or walking more than 1–2 city blocks. He has had three bursts of oral prednisone in the last 6 months and reports significant mood changes, insomnia, facial rounding, and difficulty controlling his blood pressure while on higher doses. He is very motivated to reduce his steroid exposure and has good insight into adherence, reliably using a smartphone app to track inhaler usage and symptoms.
notes__additional_comments.notes_additional_comments_line_2: Current respiratory regimen includes: budesonide-formoterol 160/4.5 two puffs BID with spacer, tiotropium 2.5 mcg two puffs daily, montelukast 10 mg nightly, albuterol HFA 2 puffs q4–6h PRN (currently using 3–4 times per week), fluticasone nasal spray 2 sprays each nostril daily, and oral prednisone 10 mg daily with a taper plan to begin 2–3 weeks after Nucala initiation if symptoms remain stable. Most recent labs (November 2025) showed eosinophils 1.6 K/µL (19%), creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, AST/ALT within normal limits, and ANCA positive (p-ANCA). Chest CT showed no acute infiltrates but did demonstrate scattered ground-glass opacities consistent with eosinophilic inflammation. Patient has reliable transportation, prefers late afternoon appointments on Tuesdays or Thursdays, and understands that he must remain in the infusion center for at least 30 minutes after each injection for observation. No history of anaphylaxis to biologics or vaccines. Please call office contact if patient misses any scheduled dose so follow-up can be arranged promptly.
